title: Waterbomb base
subtitle: One of the two primary origami bases, used in the waterbomb and balloon models
excerpt: The waterbomb base is one of the two foundational collapse structures in origami (the other is the preliminary base). It is formed by collapsing a square with two perpendicular valley folds and one diagonal mountain fold into a flat triangle. The base is used directly in the waterbomb and balloon and as an intermediate step in many more complex models.
sourceNotes: The waterbomb base and the waterbomb model are documented in Akira Yoshizawa's origami publications and in Friedrich Froebel's 19th-century paper-folding instructional series. The base is also described in Samuel Randlett, The Art of Origami (1961), and in Isao Honda, World of Origami (1965). Pre-1928 documentation of the structure exists in Froebel's Kindergarten Gifts (1826).

content.text: The
waterbomb base
is produced by making four creases in a square and then collapsing them simultaneously. Two of the four creases are
valley folds
running through the centre of the square parallel to the edges; the other two are
mountain folds

running corner to corner. When these four creases are pushed into shape simultaneously, the square collapses into a flat triangle. The base is the structural foundation for the waterbomb (a balloon that can be inflated by blowing into a small hole at the base). The
preliminary base
uses the same crease pattern with valley and mountain folds reversed.
What you need

items.qty: 1 sheet for practice; several for the full waterbomb
items.name: Square origami paper, 15 cm x 15 cm (kami, foil-backed, or plain copy paper cut square)

content.text: Making the crease pattern
Valley fold the square in half horizontally (bottom edge to top edge). Crease firmly and open back to flat.
Valley fold the square in half vertically (left edge to right edge). Crease firmly and open back to flat.
Turn the paper over. Mountain fold diagonally corner to corner (top-left to bottom-right). Crease firmly and open back.
Mountain fold the other diagonal (top-right to bottom-left). Crease firmly and open back. The paper now has four creases: two valley folds forming a cross, two mountain folds forming an X.
The collapse
Hold the paper with the coloured side up (if using coloured paper). Pinch the two opposite corners of the horizontal valley fold and push them toward each other. The paper begins to collapse inward.

As the sides come together, the top of the square folds down, and the four corners of the square meet at a single point at the bottom of the collapsed shape. Flatten the result into a triangle, with two equal flaps on each side of the centre line.
The resulting flat triangle with four layers (two flaps each side of the centre) is the waterbomb base.
Completing the waterbomb

From the waterbomb base: fold all four corner flaps of the triangle up to the apex, one at a time. Tuck the left and right flaps into the pockets formed by the corner folds. Turn the model over and blow firmly through the small hole at the bottom to inflate the balloon into a cube shape.
Where this leads
